title: The Pope, AI, and the WTO
gist: Former WTO official Alan Wolff argues that the largely dormant 'public morals' exception in WTO rules (GATT/GATS) is poised to become central to trade conflicts over AI regulation, with Pope Leo XIV's 2026 AI encyclical providing moral framing. He predicts the defining 21st-century trade fight will be ethical AI governance versus the free cross-border flow of AI goods, services, and data—pitting US tech giants against foreign regulators.

insights.claim: Wolff predicts the WTO's long-dormant 'public morals' exception—invoked in only a handful of cases over 80 years and never successfully sustained as a defense—is likely to become central to trade conflicts as governments regulate AI.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Signals a new legal battleground that could legitimize AI-related trade restrictions, reshaping market access for AI products.
insights.quote: It is this largely ignored corner of the rules of the world trading system that is likely to become central in trade conflicts in the coming years.
insights.locator: Opening, 'The main thesis of Pope Leo XIV's'

insights.claim: In all three landmark public-morals cases (US internet gambling DS285, EU seal products DS400/401, China publications DS363), the WTO found the moral objective legitimate but ruled the specific restriction unnecessary or discriminatory, so the exception has never succeeded as a litigation defense.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Establishes the legal test (necessary + non-discriminatory) AI restrictions must pass; precedent has been a high bar, but Wolff expects that to change.
insights.quote: As a result, the exception has never successfully been raised as a defense of a regulation in litigation at the WTO. That result is likely to change.

themes.theme: AI standards as the live front

insights.claim: AI standards disputes are already surfacing through the WTO's pre-adoption TBT 'specific trade concerns' process: at the November 2025 TBT Committee meeting members discussed 70 STCs, 12 of them AI-related, covering AI conformity assessment, algorithm transparency, cybersecurity, data governance, and digital-product regulation.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Quantifies the early surge in AI trade friction; the pre-litigation STC channel, not court cases, is where AI-standard conflicts first appear.
insights.quote: members discussed 70 STCs, 12 of which have been referred to as AI-related, including AI conformity assessment, algorithm transparency requirements, cybersecurity rules, data governance, and digital-product regulation
insights.locator: 'The WTO reports that at least 20 new AI-related concerns'

insights.claim: Wolff predicts the volume of AI-related specific trade concerns will grow dramatically, pitting US tech giants against regulators in many foreign capitals.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Frames AI regulation as a structural, escalating US-vs-foreign-regulator conflict—a persistent overhang for global tech market access.
insights.quote: Standards governing AI are likely to pit the US tech giants against the regulators in many foreign capitals.

insights.claim: AI regulation is fragmenting globally: Brazil's Bill No. 2338/2023 would fine violators up to 2% of Brazilian revenue or ban AI operations; South Korea and China already have AI laws; and Chile, Mexico, and Argentina are considering legislation.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Divergent national AI rules raise compliance cost and market-access risk for AI providers; the 2%-of-revenue penalty sets a tangible downside.
insights.quote: Violations can result in severe consequences, including fines of up to 2 percent of a company's revenue in Brazil or complete bans on AI operations.

insights.claim: Wolff notes the array of AI-related trade measures already deployed includes hardware tariffs on semiconductors and servers, export controls, IP protection, and dual-use regulations.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Lists the concrete policy toolkit affecting AI hardware supply chains beyond the abstract WTO debate.
insights.quote: Hardware tariffs (on semiconductors and servers), export controls, protection of intellectual property, and dual use regulations, are among the measures being deployed.

insights.claim: Wolff argues the defining 21st-century trade issue will be neither Trump's tariffs nor middle powers wresting control from the US and China, but the contest between ethical AI regulation and the unencumbered cross-border flow of AI goods, services, and data.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Reframes the long-run trade narrative away from tariff headlines toward AI governance—a strategic lens for positioning around tech-trade policy.
insights.quote: Rather, it is likely to be the contest between the ethical regulation of AI and the unencumbered development and cross-border flow of AI-related goods and services.
insights.locator: 'The defining issues in international trade relations'

insights.claim: Anthropic co-founder Christopher Olah, present at the encyclical's release, said AI companies need external moral voices because their incentives and constraints can conflict with doing the right thing.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Signals AI-industry openness to external ethical governance, relevant to how willingly firms may accept regulation-driven trade constraints.
insights.quote: We need moral voices that the incentives cannot bend
insights.locator: 'Christopher Olah, a co-founder of the AI company Anthropic'
